fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that RPC server wrapper thing we discussed last sprint. The basic idea is: clients send requests in that standard JSON-based RPC format (you know the one, 2.0 spec), and our server needs to route them to the right backend operation and send back a properly shaped response. We also need it to work over plain byte streams AND HTTP — the HTTP side needs to return the right status codes depending on what happened (the usual suspects: success, not found, bad input, server error, etc.).

One thing that tripped us up with the old login module compatibility logic — make sure whatever dispatch mechanism we use handles methods that have the same name but different argument signatures. Clients might also send a whole bundle of requests at once in one shot, and we need to handle that gracefully.

Also critical: when backend services blow up, we absolutely cannot leak any internal Java class names or stack traces back to the client. Map errors to clean numeric codes only.

There's also a testing shim/adapter we need — something that reads a single JSON blob from stdin describing the scenario, runs the engine, and prints out a normalized line-by-line view of the result to stdout so our CI pipeline can diff it. The adapter needs to support a flag that lets callers say 'I know I'm sending extra/fewer args than expected, that's fine.' Refer to the existing parameter flexibility logic we discussed for details on how permissive mode should work.

A couple extra specifics from the questions that came in: if the incoming JSON is malformed or just empty, that should come back as error code -32700 with category 'parse_error' and message 'JSON parse error', and in that case the id needs to be rendered as the string "null", so id="null". On the HTTP side, the status mapping should be straightforward: ok/success → 200, method_not_found → 404, parse_error/invalid_request → 400, and method_params_invalid/internal_error/error_not_handled/bulk_error/custom server codes → 500. Also, if it’s a batch and even one sub-request fails, the overall HTTP response should still be 500.

For the normalized adapter output, for HTTP transports (http_post, http_get), start with 'http_status=<int>' and 'content_type=application/json-rpc' before any body fields. If there isn’t actually a body, like a notification over HTTP, render that as the single line 'body=empty'. Also on the error formatting side, error_data only gets printed if it exists. If the value is just a plain string, print it as 'error_data=<text>'. If it’s a structured/object value that carries a message field, print it as 'error_data_message=<text>' instead. If no data was set, neither one should appear.

Also wanted to be explicit about the arg-count flexibility knobs since that came up a few times. The two boolean flags are 'allowExtraParams' and 'allowLessParams', both default false. 'allowExtraParams' permits and silently ignores surplus positional arguments. 'allowLessParams' permits missing trailing arguments, which are bound to their type default/null. If those flags are not enabled, any argument count mismatch should return error code -32602 (method_params_invalid) with message 'method parameters invalid'.

And on the same-name-methods piece, the selection rule is by both parameter count and parameter types, not just the name. For positional params, types are inferred from JSON value types (string vs integer). For named params (object), matching is by parameter name set and types. The result string should reflect which variant was actually picked, like 'overloaded:string:x' vs 'overloaded:int:7'. Same deal in the dispatch engine generally: when multiple operations share a method name, choose the one that matches parameter count and JSON value types (string vs integer), following the service's overloadedMethod variants and the engine's argument binding policy.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: void main(String[] args) throws Exception — Adapter reads one JSON case-input object from System.in, drives the JsonRpcServer engine, writes normalized key=value lines to System.out

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A request with no 'id' field is a notification. If it succeeds, the engine produces NO response at all — the adapter must output only the single token 'no_response'. A void operation (returns nothing) yields a JSON null result in the response, e.g. result=null.

trigger_keywords: how to hide exception class names
stack trace leaking in error response
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Host-language artifacts — Java exception class names, fully-qualified type names, stack traces, runtime-specific message suffixes — must NEVER appear in any error_message or error_data output field. Only the numeric error_code, derived error_category, and any explicitly configured message/data strings are allowed.

Batch responses are emitted in ascending numeric 'id' order, regardless of the order requests appeared in the input array. Output starts with 'batch_size=N', then each entry prefixed by a '--' separator line, followed by that entry's standard key=value fields.

trigger_keywords: what category for non-standard error codes
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Any error code that does not map to a recognized standard category (parse_error=-32700, invalid_request=-32600, method_not_found=-32601, method_params_invalid=-32602, internal_error=-32603, error_not_handled=-32001, bulk_error=-32002) is reported with error_category='custom'.

trigger_keywords: original method name still works after aliasing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An operation may be published under an external alias (e.g., 'custom.echo' for the method 'customEcho'). Both the alias AND the original declared method name remain valid and dispatchable. The alias is registered at service configuration time.

trigger_keywords: what fields does the adapter stdin JSON accept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The adapter stdin JSON supports: 'transport' (raw/http_post/http_get, default raw), 'request' (the parsed JSON-RPC object or batch array), 'request_raw' (exact bytes string for malformed input testing), 'query' (for http_get: object with method/id/params keys), 'allowExtraParams' (bool), 'allowLessParams' (bool), and 'statusProvider': "custom" to activate a pluggable HTTP status code remapper.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that standard JSON-based RPC format (you know the one, 2.0 spec)
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol specification: request objects with jsonrpc, id, method, params fields; response objects with result or error; notifications (no id); batch requests (JSON arrays); standardized error codes defined in the table in start.md.

context_pointers.vague_reference: send a whole bundle of requests at once in one shot
context_pointers.actual_meaning: JSON-RPC 2.0 batch processing: the client sends a JSON array of request objects; the engine processes each independently and returns a JSON array of correlated responses, sorted by ascending id, output with batch_size=N header and -- separators.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify from the business side — please check the PRD (start.md) for the technical specification, or look at the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for concrete input/output examples of that behavior.

feature1_positional_dispatch.json.description: Positional dispatch: a request with an array of params is routed to the matching method by position; the result envelope echoes the request id (integer, string, or large integer), a void method yields a null result, and a request without an id (a notification) that succeeds produces no response at all.

cases.description: echo a string value positionally
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="hello"

cases.description: integer result from a two-argument call
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result=5

feature2_parameter_arity.json.description: Parameter arity validation for positional calls: supplying too few or too many positional arguments is rejected with the method-parameters-invalid error, unless the server is configured to allow missing (allowLessParams) or extra (allowExtraParams) arguments, in which case the call proceeds.

cases.description: too few positional arguments is rejected
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=error
error_code=-32602
error_category=method_params_invalid
error_message=method parameters invalid

cases.description: too many positional arguments is rejected
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=error
error_code=-32602
error_category=method_params_invalid
error_message=method parameters invalid

feature3_overload_resolution.json.description: Overload resolution by argument count and type: when several methods share a name, the engine selects the variant whose parameter count and types match the supplied positional arguments (no args, one string, one integer, two strings, or two integers).

cases.description: no-argument overload
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="overloaded:0"

cases.description: single string-argument overload
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="overloaded:string:x"

feature4_named_parameters.json.description: Named-parameter dispatch: a request whose params is an object is matched to a method by parameter names, including overload selection by the named set and type; argument values are coerced to the declared parameter types, and a value that cannot be coerced is rejected with the method-parameters-invalid error identifying the offending argument position.

cases.description: named object selects the single-string overload
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="overloaded:string:x"

cases.description: named object selects the single-integer overload
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="overloaded:int:7"

feature5_custom_method_name.json.description: Custom external method names: a method may be published under an alias; the alias dispatches to the implementation, and the method also remains invocable by its original declared name.

cases.description: invoking by the registered alias succeeds
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="custom:yo"

cases.description: the original declared name remains invocable
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="custom:yo"

feature6_protocol_errors.json.description: Protocol-level error reporting: malformed JSON input is reported as a parse error, an empty-line payload is also a parse error, and a request naming an unknown method is reported as method-not-found.

cases.description: malformed JSON yields a parse error
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id="null"
outcome=error
error_code=-32700
error_category=parse_error
error_message=JSON parse error

cases.description: empty-line payload yields a parse error
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id="null"
outcome=error
error_code=-32700
error_category=parse_error
error_message=JSON parse error

feature7_error_mapping.json.description: Service-error mapping: an unmapped exception thrown by a method becomes a generic error-not-handled response, while exceptions explicitly mapped to a code are reported with that code; an explicit message and data override the defaults, and when no message override is given the thrown exception's own message is surfaced. Host-language class names are never exposed.

cases.description: unmapped exception becomes error-not-handled
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=error
error_code=-32001
error_category=error_not_handled

cases.description: mapped exception reports the configured code
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=error
error_code=1234
error_category=custom

cases.description: explicit message and data override the defaults
cases.expected_output:
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=error
error_code=-5678
error_category=custom
error_message=The message
error_data=The data

feature8_batch_processing.json.description: Batch processing: an array of requests produces an array of correlated responses, each keyed to its request id; successful and failing sub-requests coexist in one batch, each carrying its own result or error.

cases.description: all sub-requests succeed
cases.expected_output:
batch_size=2
--
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="a"
--
jsonrpc=2.0
id=2
outcome=success
result="overloaded:int:5"

cases.description: mixed success and error in one batch
cases.expected_output:
batch_size=3
--
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="overloaded:string:a"
--
jsonrpc=2.0
id=2
outcome=error
error_code=-32001
error_category=error_not_handled
--
jsonrpc=2.0
id=3
outcome=success
result="c"

feature9_http_status.json.description: HTTP transport status mapping: over HTTP the response carries a status code derived from the JSON-RPC outcome - success is 200, an unknown method is 404, malformed input is 400, and a service or parameter error is 500; a batch containing any error reports 500. GET requests build the call from method/id/params query parameters, and a custom status-code provider can remap every category.

cases.description: successful call over HTTP is 200
cases.expected_output:
http_status=200
content_type=application/json-rpc
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=success
result="hi"

cases.description: unknown method over HTTP is 404
cases.expected_output:
http_status=404
content_type=application/json-rpc
jsonrpc=2.0
id=1
outcome=error
error_code=-32601
error_category=method_not_found
error_message=method not found

cases.description: malformed input over HTTP is 400
cases.expected_output:
http_status=400
content_type=application/json-rpc
jsonrpc=2.0
id="null"
outcome=error
error_code=-32700
error_category=parse_error
error_message=JSON parse error
